did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a weighted ensemble transfer learning approach combining seven deep learning neural networks (InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and others) to perform binary classification of skin lesions (Melanoma vs. Nevus) using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a weighted ensemble transfer learning approach for melanoma classification. It details the methodology using seven deep learning models (InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and a custom CNN), describes the use of the ISIC dataset for experimentation, and reports specific quantitative results (e.g., accuracy of 93.36% on the first ISIC dataset and 85.54% on another). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to introduce and validate a new technical method with original results.

title: A weighted ensemble transfer learning approach for melanoma classification from skin lesion images
brief_summary: This study proposes a weighted ensemble transfer learning approach to perform binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma and Nevus categories. The methodology combines predictions from seven deep learning neural networks, including InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and a custom CNN, with weights determined by their individual testing accuracies. The models were experimentally evaluated using the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset. On the primary ISIC dataset, the proposed ensemble method achieved an accuracy of 93.36%, along with 93% precision, recall, and F1-score, and 97% sensitivity and specificity. The robustness of the approach was further validated on a second ISIC dataset, where it attained an accuracy of 85.54%. These results demonstrate that the weighted ensemble technique outperforms individual models and compares favorably with state-of-the-art techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: Binary classification target class in the ISIC dataset experiment. Assigned label 0. Total count in dataset: 5106 (4059 train, 1047 test).
Binary classification target class in the ISIC dataset experiment. Assigned label 1. Total count in dataset: 6343 (5100 train, 1243 test).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma and Nevus categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification using a weighted ensemble transfer learning approach. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma vs. Nevus) from the ISIC dataset. The final decision is made by selecting the class with the highest predicted probability from the weighted sum of outputs from seven deep learning models (InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and a custom CNN).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019/2020 Melanoma Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin lesion images obtained from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/qikangdeng/isic-2019-and-2020-melanoma-dataset). It contains dermoscopy images in .jpg format.

use.training: Used for training the seven deep learning models (InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and custom CNN) with 80% of the data.
use.testing: Used for testing the models with 20% of the data to determine weights for the ensemble and report performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.).

datasets_used.name: Second ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: Another ISIC dataset used to assess the robustness of the proposed weighted ensemble classifier. Obtained from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/jaiahuja/skin-cancer-detection).

use.testing: Used for external validation/testing to evaluate the robustness of the model trained on the first ISIC dataset. The ensemble achieved an accuracy of 85.54% on this dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, specificity) and comparative tables/graphs without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset 1 (Primary).description: Binary classification of Melanoma (5106 images) and Nevus (6343 images) using 80:20 train-test split.
ISIC Dataset 1 (Primary).notes: EfficientNetB4 achieved the highest accuracy among individual models. The weighted ensemble model outperformed the average ensemble model. AUC values for individual models ranged from 0.785 (InceptionV3) to 0.917 (EfficientNetB4).

ISIC Dataset 2 (Robustness/Secondary).description: Binary classification of Melanoma (454 images) and Nevus (373 images) from a different source (Kaggle: jaiahuja/skin-cancer-detection).
ISIC Dataset 2 (Robustness/Secondary).notes: ResNet50 was the best-performing individual algorithm on this dataset. The weighted ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 85.54%, demonstrating robustness.

architecture_used.main_model: Weighted Ensemble of seven deep learning models: InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and a custom CNN.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used. Models were pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset. The custom CNN was self-constructed.

architecture_details.InceptionV3: Includes convolutions, average pooling, max pooling, concatenations, dropouts, and fully connected layers. Uses label smoothing and factorized 7x7 convolutions. Auxiliary classifier used.
architecture_details.MobileNet: 53-layer deep CNN architecture targeted for mobile/embedded vision. Uses depth-wise separable convolutions.
architecture_details.Xception: 71-layer CNN with same parameters as Inception V3 but more effective use of model parameters.
architecture_details.VGG16: Deep CNN with 16 layers. Imported from pre-trained ImageNet database.
architecture_details.ResNet50: 48 layers along with 1-1 max-pool and average pool layer. Uses residual blocks/skip links.
architecture_details.EfficientNetB4: CNN architecture that evenly balances depth, breadth, and resolution dimensions using a compound coefficient.
architecture_details.Custom CNN: 4-layer deep CNN. Kernel size 3x3. Max pooling on layers 2 and 4. Dropout of 0.25 on layers 2-4. Two dense layers with dropout of 0.5. ReLU activation in hidden layers, Sigmoid in dense layers.

training_details.epochs: Convergence observed after 76 epochs for EfficientNetB4 and ResNet50.

activation_functions.ensemble_aggregation: Softmax (used for calculating loss/probabilities in individual models)

training_details.normalization: Batch normalization applied to activation inputs.

classification_or_output_head.method: Weighted Ensemble. Weights determined by individual model testing accuracy. Final prediction is the class with the highest probability from the weighted sum of outputs.

preprocessing_techniques: Label encoding (converting labels to numeric format: Melanoma=0, Nevus=1)
conclusions: The proposed weighted ensemble transfer learning approach, combining seven deep learning models (InceptionV3, VGG16, Xception, ResNet50, EfficientNetB4, MobileNet, and a custom CNN), effectively performs binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma and Nevus categories.

On the primary ISIC dataset, the weighted ensemble classifier achieved superior performance with an accuracy of 93.36%, precision of 93%, recall of 93%, F1-score of 93%, sensitivity of 97%, and specificity of 97%.
The weighted ensemble method outperformed individual deep learning models and an average ensemble model on the primary ISIC dataset.
The methodology demonstrated robustness when evaluated on a second, distinct ISIC dataset, achieving an accuracy of 85.54%.

Among individual models on the primary ISIC dataset, EfficientNetB4 achieved the highest accuracy (91.79%), while on the second ISIC dataset, ResNet50 was the best-performing individual algorithm (78.31% accuracy).

The study concludes that computer-aided diagnostic tools using weighted ensemble transfer learning offer a more objective and reliable alternative to subjective manual dermoscopy evaluation for early melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nevus), ignoring other critical skin lesion types (e.g., BCC, SCC) present in the ISIC dataset, which limits clinical applicability for general dermatology.

The secondary validation dataset (ISIC 2) is small (827 images) and sourced from a different Kaggle user (jaiahuja), raising concerns about data provenance, potential overlap with the primary dataset, and generalizability.

The primary dataset was obtained from a non-official Kaggle source (qikangdeng), not directly from the ISIC challenge archives, which may introduce bias or quality inconsistencies compared to the official ISIC 2019/2020 challenge data.

No data leakage prevention strategies (e.g., patient-level splitting) are reported; with dermoscopy images, multiple images from the same patient may exist in both train and test sets, potentially inflating performance metrics.

The paper lacks statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) for the reported performance improvements, making it difficult to assess if the weighted ensemble's superiority over individual models is statistically robust.
The input image resolution is very low (100x100 pixels), which may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for melanoma detection compared to standard clinical resolutions.
No data augmentation techniques were employed, relying solely on the existing dataset size, which may limit the model's ability to generalize to diverse skin tones and lesion presentations.

The weights for the ensemble were determined based on testing accuracy, which constitutes a form of data leakage if the 'testing' set used for weight calculation was not held out from the final evaluation or if it influenced the training process indirectly.
The study does not report performance across different Fitzpatrick skin types, a known source of bias in dermatological AI, potentially limiting equity and clinical relevance for darker skin tones.
